var_functions.query_db:12.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:12.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. args.code:
import json
import re

# Load all business data
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:30']

# Extract categories from business descriptions
category_counts = {}
business_categories = {}

for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    description = business.get('description', '')
    
    # Extract categories from description (look for text after "services in" or "including")
    categories = []
    
    # Pattern 1: "services in Cat1, Cat2, and Cat3"
    match1 = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
    if match1:
        text = match1.group(1)
        # Split by commas and 'and'
        parts = re.split(r',|\band\b', text)
        categories = [part.strip() for part in parts if part.strip()]
    
    # Pattern 2: "including Cat1, Cat2, and Cat3"
    if not categories:
        match2 = re.search(r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match2:
            text = match2.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',|\band\b', text)
            categories = [part.strip() for part in parts if part.strip()]
    
    # Pattern 3: "offers a range of services including Cat1, Cat2"
    if not categories:
        match3 = re.search(r'range of services including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match3:
            text = match3.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',|\band\b', text)
            categories = [part.strip() for part in parts if part.strip()]
    
    # Store mapping from business_id to categories
    business_categories[business_id] = categories

# Show some examples
for i, (bid, cats) in enumerate(list(business_categories.items())[:5]):
    print(f"{bid}: {cats}")

print(f"\nTotal businesses with parsed categories: {len(business_categories)}")

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load data
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:30']

# Extract categories from business descriptions
business_categories = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    description = business.get('description', '')
    
    categories = []
    # Look for patterns like "services in X, Y, and Z" or "including X, Y, and Z"
    for pattern in [r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', r'range of services including (.+?)(?:\.|$)']:
        match = re.search(pattern, description, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            # Split by commas and 'and'
            parts = re.split(r',|\band\b', text)
            categories = [part.strip() for part in parts if part.strip()]
            break
    
    business_categories[business_id] = categories

# Map business_ref to business_id
# business_ref: "businessref_45" -> business_id: "businessid_45"
ref_to_id = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    # Extract number from business_id
    num_match = re.search(r'businessid_(\d+)', business_id)
    if num_match:
        num = num_match.group(1)
        ref_to_id[f'businessref_{num}'] = business_id

# Count reviews per category
category_review_counts = {}
for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    
    # Get business_id for this review
    if business_ref in ref_to_id:
        business_id = ref_to_id[business_ref]
        
        # Get categories for this business
        categories = business_categories.get(business_id, [])
        
        # Count review for each category
        for category in categories:
            category_review_counts[category] = category_review_counts.get(category, 0) + 1

# Sort categories by review count
top_categories = sorted(category_review_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:5]

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(top_categories))

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load data
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:30']

# Extract all categories from business descriptions
all_categories = []
for business in businesses:
    desc = business.get('description', '')
    # Look for category patterns
    for pattern in [r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', r'range of services including (.+?)(?:\.|$)']:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            categories = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',|\band\b', text)]
            categories = [c.rstrip('.') for c in categories if c.strip() and c.strip().lower() != 'and']
            all_categories.extend(categories)
            break

# Count review per category more robustly
category_review_counts = {}
for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    
    # Find corresponding business
    for business in businesses:
        # Extract numbers from business_id and business_ref
        bid_match = re.search(r'businessid_(\d+)', business['business_id'])
        ref_match = re.search(r'businessref_(\d+)', business_ref)
        
        if bid_match and ref_match and bid_match.group(1) == ref_match.group(1):
            desc = business.get('description', '')
            
            # Extract categories for this business
            categories = []
            for pattern in [r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', r'range of services including (.+?)(?:\.|$)']:
                match = re.search(pattern, desc, re.IGNORECASE)
                if match:
                    text = match.group(1)
                    cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',|\band\b', text)]
                    categories = [c.rstrip('.') for c in cats if c.strip() and c.strip().lower() != 'and']
                    break
            
            # Count reviews for each category
            for category in categories:
                category_review_counts[category] = category_review_counts.get(category, 0) + 1
            break

# Sort and get top 5
top_5_categories = sorted(category_review_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:5]

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(top_5_categories))

var_functions.query_db:12.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:12.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:12.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load data
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:54']

# Map business ids
business_lookup = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    num = re.search(r'businessid_(\d+)', business_id).group(1)
    business_lookup[num] = business

# Count reviews per business
business_review_counts = {}
for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    num = re.search(r'businessref_(\d+)', business_ref).group(1)
    business_review_counts[num] = business_review_counts.get(num, 0) + 1

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    "business_lookup_keys": list(business_lookup.keys()),
    "reviewed_business_numbers": list(business_review_counts.keys()),
    "top_reviewed": sorted(business_review_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:10]
}))

import json
import re

# Load data
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:54']

# Map business ids
business_lookup = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    num = re.search(r'businessid_(\d+)', business_id).group(1)
    business_lookup[num] = business

# Count reviews per business
business_review_counts = {}
for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    num = re.search(r'businessref_(\d+)', business_ref).group(1)
    business_review_counts[num] = business_review_counts.get(num, 0) + 1

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    "business_lookup_keys": list(business_lookup.keys()),
    "reviewed_business_numbers": list(business_review_counts.keys()),
    "top_reviewed": sorted(business_review_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:10]
}))

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load data
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:54']

# Map business numbers to their data
business_data = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    num = re.search(r'businessid_(\d+)', business_id).group(1)
    categories = []
    
    desc = business.get('description', '')
    for pattern in [r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', r'range of services including (.+?)(?:\.|$)']:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',|\band\b', text)]
            categories = [c.rstrip('.') for c in cats if c.strip() and c.strip().lower() not in ['and', 'or']]
            break
    
    business_data[num] = {
        'name': business['name'],
        'categories': categories
    }

# Count reviews per business number
review_counts = {}
for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    num = re.search(r'businessref_(\d+)', business_ref).group(1)
    review_counts[num] = review_counts.get(num, 0) + 1

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = {}
for num, count in review_counts.items():
    if num in business_data:
        for category in business_data[num]['categories']:
            category_counts[category] = category_counts.get(category, 0) + count

# Sort and get top 5
top_5 = sorted(category_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:5]

# Also include total counts for context
total_reviews = sum(review_counts.values())
categorized_reviews = sum(category_counts.values())

result = {
    "top_5_categories": top_5,
    "total_businesses_reviewed": len(review_counts),
    "total_businesses_in_db": len(business_data),
    "total_reviews": total_reviews,
    "reviews_with_category_info": categorized_reviews
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Load data
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:54']

# Map business numbers to their data
business_data = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    num = re.search(r'businessid_(\d+)', business_id).group(1)
    categories = []
    
    desc = business.get('description', '')
    for pattern in [r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', r'range of services including (.+?)(?:\.|$)']:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',|\band\b', text)]
            categories = [c.rstrip('.') for c in cats if c.strip() and c.strip().lower() not in ['and', 'or']]
            break
    
    business_data[num] = {
        'name': business['name'],
        'categories': categories
    }

# Count reviews per business number
review_counts = {}
for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    num = re.search(r'businessref_(\d+)', business_ref).group(1)
    review_counts[num] = review_counts.get(num, 0) + 1

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = {}
for num, count in review_counts.items():
    if num in business_data:
        for category in business_data[num]['categories']:
            category_counts[category] = category_counts.get(category, 0) + count

# Sort and get top 5
top_5 = sorted(category_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:5]

# Also include total counts for context
total_reviews = sum(review_counts.values())
categorized_reviews = sum(category_counts.values())

result = {
    "top_5_categories": top_5,
    "total_businesses_reviewed": len(review_counts),
    "total_businesses_in_db": len(business_data),
    "total_reviews": total_reviews,
    "reviews_with_category_info": categorized_reviews
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:12.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!
